headline: How Dropbox uses S3 Transfer Acceleration, Multipart Upload, and Cross-Region Replication
why: Dropbox users in Tokyo, São Paulo, and Berlin all upload files to the same platform. Without Transfer Acceleration and smart upload strategies, a 2GB file upload from Japan to a US bucket would be painfully slow.

topics.usage: Any Dropbox file over 100MB is split into parts and uploaded in parallel. A 4GB video upload splits into 80× 50MB parts — if part 47 fails, only that part retries. The file appears in the user's Dropbox as soon as the last part completes.

Dropbox enables Transfer Acceleration on its upload buckets. A user in Tokyo uploads to the nearest AWS edge location (Tokyo), which routes through AWS's private backbone to the US-East-1 bucket — up to 60% faster than uploading directly.

Dropbox Business customers' files are replicated from US-East-1 to EU-West-1 automatically via CRR. Ensures business continuity if an AWS region goes down, and satisfies GDPR data residency for European enterprise clients.

Dropbox runs S3 Inventory reports nightly to find files not accessed in 90+ days, then Lifecycle rules move them to Standard-IA. Files not accessed for 1 year move to Glacier. Users don't notice — they just pay less.

diagram:
sequenceDiagram
    participant User as 👤 User (Tokyo)
    participant Edge as AWS Edge (Tokyo)
    participant Backbone as AWS Backbone
    participant S3 as S3 Bucket (US-East-1)
    participant EU as S3 Replica (EU-West-1)
    User->>Edge: Upload part 1..N (Transfer Accel)
    Edge->>Backbone: Fast private network
    Backbone->>S3: Assemble multipart
    S3->>EU: Cross-Region Replication
    Note over S3,EU: Auto-replicated for DR + GDPR
takeaway: Dropbox's upload pipeline uses three S3 features simultaneously: Multipart for reliability, Transfer Acceleration for speed, and Cross-Region Replication for compliance. Each feature solves a different problem for the same file.
